Query: What is the EU Deforestation Regulation and how does it affect German timber exporters?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — The EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR, Regulation (EU) 2023/1115) requires German timber exporters to prove that their products are deforestation-free and legally produced, with compliance enforced by the Federal Office for Agriculture and Food as of early 2026.

The EUDR covers wood and timber products, among other commodities, and mandates that companies placing, supplying, or exporting these products must demonstrate that they were not produced on land deforested after December 31, 2020, and that all relevant legal requirements in the country of production were met. Companies must submit a due diligence declaration and track their supply chains to ensure compliance. Violations can result in heavy fines, product confiscation, and exclusion from the EU market.

The regulation replaces the previous EU Timber Regulation (EUTR, No. 995/2010) and introduces binding corporate due diligence obligations. In Germany, the Federal Office for Agriculture and Food (BLE) is responsible for implementation, providing detailed guidance and resources for affected companies. The German timber trade welcomed the EUDR, noting that the obligation to submit due diligence declarations now rests solely with companies first placing products on the EU market, not downstream traders. The EU Commission is expected to present a report on administrative burdens by the end of April 2026.

Details can be found in the DIHK statement on the review of the EUDR dated 30 January 2026 (PDF, 221 KB)  (only available in German)

### Where can I learn more?

The Federal Office for Agriculture and Food (BLE), which implements the EUDR in Germany, answers questions at anfragen@entwaldungsfreie-produkte.de and has compiled all the information about the regulation on its website: Who is affected? How does the EU information system work? What needs to be considered when filing a customs declaration? Diagrams, explanatory videos, FAQs, event notes, legal bases, and much more can be found at www.ble.de (only available in German). [...] The regulation requires companies to prove that these products were not produced on land deforested after December 31, 2020, that the production adhered to the legal regulations of the producing country, and that they have a declaration of due diligence.

Companies are subject to EUDR's rules if they place, supply, or export specific raw materials (cattle, cocoa, coffee, oil palm, rubber, soy, or timber) or related products within or from the EU. [...] For the smallest and small primary producers from low-risk countries, a one-time registration in the EU system and a simplified declaration of due diligence is sufficient.

Books, newspapers and printed materials are excluded from the product scope of the EUDR.

## ... and Review

Finally, the Council has instructed the EU Commission to examine further relief options by April 2026.

In January 2026, DIHK commented on this review with a catalogue of demands. The range of proposals extends from the elimination of the archiving obligation for reference numbers for first downstream market participants and traders to linking the regulation with accompanying trade and development policy measures.

# EU Regulation on Deforestation-free Products (EUDR)

The Regulation on Deforestation-free Products (EUDR), Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, regulates trade in certain agricultural and timber products: Import, export and trade on the EU market are only permitted if products are demonstrably deforestation-free and legal. The EU aims to minimise the part played by European consumption in global deforestation by introducing binding corporate due diligence obligations. These obligations will affect the following raw materials: wood, cocoa, coffee, palm oil, rubber, beef and soya, as well as products made from them. Violations can result in heavy fines, product confiscation and exclusion from the market. [...] The regulation requires companies to track their supply chains. The competent authority for implementation in Germany is the Federal Office for Agriculture and Food. The EUDR will replace the earlier EUTR (EU Regulation No. 995/2010, EU Timber Regulation). Special rules apply during a transitional period until 2028, for which the date of harvesting and placing on the market are decisive.

## Actors & obligations

The Regulation (in its current version) distinguishes between different economic operators with different obligations. The responsibilities of companies vary depending on their role, size and position in the supply chain.

The postponement of the start of application will give the affected economic operators, producers in third countries and the competent authorities of the EU Member States more time to prepare for the application of the EUDR.

ground_truth: The EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR, Regulation EU 2023/1115) requires that timber and other commodity products traded on the EU market are demonstrably deforestation-free and legally produced. Implementation has been postponed twice by the end of 2025. The German timber trade association GD Holz welcomed the revision of the EUDR, calling it a success for the entire wood value chain and stating it provides practical solutions that protect forests without overburdening companies. Companies must implement due diligence systems and submit documentation to prove compliance. The Federal Office for Agriculture and Food (BLE) is the designated German enforcement authority.

Query: What are the current building permit trends in Germany in 2026?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — German building permits for residential construction rose sharply in January 2026, with an 8.4% year-on-year increase to 19,500 units, marking the first significant growth after several years of decline.

According to Fordaq and Finanznachrichten, permits for new-build housing in January 2026 increased by 7.4% to 16,400 units, while approvals for dwellings created through conversion of existing buildings climbed by 13.5% to 3,100 units. Across all residential building types, permits rose by 6.0% compared to January 2025. The strongest growth was seen in single-family houses, up 12.6%, and two-family houses, which jumped by 26.1%. Multi-family building permits, which make up the largest share of new housing, increased by 7.1% year-on-year.

Regionally, Hamburg saw a notable boost in permit numbers, with nearly 750 additional approvals attributed to new federal legislation known as the "Bau-Turbo," as reported by Stern and Abendblatt. This policy change has been credited by city officials with accelerating the permitting process and enabling hundreds of new housing units.

Industry sources, including the Zentralverband Deutsches Baugewerbe (ZDB) and the Deutsches Institut für Wirtschaftsforschung (DIW), confirm that 2026 marks a tentative turnaround for the German construction sector. DIW projects a 1.7% increase in total construction volume for 2026 after a 1.2% decline in 2025, while ZDB expects residential construction revenue to rise from €54 billion in 2025 to €56.3 billion in 2026. However, multiple sources caution that this recovery remains fragile, with risks from rising energy prices and potential interest rate hikes linked to geopolitical instability, particularly the Iran conflict.

Permits for residential construction rose 8.4% year-on-year to 19,500 units in January 2026 — making early engagement with permitting authorities and monitoring policy changes like the "Bau-Turbo" critical for project planning.

Query: What role does the German Heating Act play in shaping wood pellet demand?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — Germany’s Heating Act, as part of the broader Energiewende policy, drives wood pellet demand by promoting renewable energy heating, with government subsidies and regulations underpinning steady consumption as of March 2026.

Germany’s wood pellet fuel market is strongly shaped by the Energiewende policy, which aggressively promotes renewable energy sources for heating and power generation. Sources confirm that government subsidies for renewable heating, including those aligned with the Heating Act, have bolstered wood pellet consumption. Companies such as Graanul Invest and German Pellets have contributed to a robust production base, while sustainable forestry practices ensure a steady supply of raw materials.

In 2023, Germany produced 3.3 million tonnes of biomass pellets, supporting both residential pellet stove adoption and biomass district heating systems. Stringent environmental regulations and subsidies for biomass-fired boilers and CHP plants further accelerate the transition to wood pellets for heating, particularly in district heating networks and commercial buildings.

Query: How do European Central Bank interest rate decisions affect the German construction market?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — European Central Bank (ECB) interest rate decisions directly affect German construction by influencing mortgage rates, which in turn impact housing demand and building activity, as seen in early 2026 when mortgage rates climbed to their highest levels since mid-2024, dampening demand.

In January 2026, German mortgage rates reached their highest point since mid-2024, which sources confirm led to reduced demand for housing loans. According to ING Think, this was a direct result of capital market movements and ECB rate expectations, with German 10-year yields rising by more than 30 basis points since late February 2026, likely pushing lending rates back above 4%. The ING analysis highlights that in 2022, a rapid series of ECB rate hikes caused mortgage rates to surge from near-zero to over 3.5%, bringing the German housing market to a near standstill. For 2026, most anticipated ECB rate hikes are already priced in, and while no further hikes are expected in the base case, any additional tightening would likely have a less severe effect than in 2022.

Building permit data from Holzkurier shows that 19,500 new housing units were approved in Germany in January 2026, an increase of 8.4%, indicating some resilience in construction activity despite higher financing costs. However, the ifo Institute reports a continued high rate of order cancellations in residential construction, reflecting ongoing sensitivity to interest rate changes and financing conditions.
context_1: A temporary setback at the beginning of 2026 Early 2026 developments have been mixed. In January, mortgage rates climbed to their highest levels since mid 2024, dampening demand. February briefly brought relief: lower capital market rates reduced financing costs, which could have spurred a short term rebound in loan demand. But this window closed quickly as the escalation in the Middle East pushed energy prices higher, reignited inflation fears, and triggered a sharp sell off in government bond markets. German 10yr yields have risen by more than 30bp since late February – probably enough to push lending rates back above 4% going forward. [...] Why this is not like 2022 The current macro environment feels uncomfortable and brings back painful memories of 2022, when rising energy prices exacerbated inflation, policy rates were raised at an unprecedented pace, and the German housing market practically came to a standstill. However, in 2022, the rate shock hit a housing market that was at the end of a long boom period, with average mortgage rates surging from near-zero to more than 3.5%. At the current juncture, mortgage interest rates are already much higher and even in a worst-case scenario, most of the expected rate hikes from the European Central Bank are already priced in. While we don’t expect the ECB to hike rates at all in our base case scenario, even some monetary policy tightening would fall shy of the rate shock we saw [...] Market interest rates and lending rates (%) Source: LSEG Datastream; ING Economic & Financial Analysis THINK economic and financial analysis Article | 25 March 2026 3 As a result, the recovery in the housing market is likely to face a temporary setback. Elevated uncertainty, rising energy costs and weaker consumer confidence will probably lead to an increase in households’ propensity to save, while higher financing costs will add further financial strain.

Query: What impact is bark beetle damage having on timber supply in German forests?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — Bark beetle outbreaks have damaged over 360 million cubic meters of timber in Central Europe since 2017, with Germany heavily affected, leading to ongoing supply shortages and discounts of €20/m³ for beetle-damaged wood as of Q1 2026.

The catastrophic spruce bark beetle outbreak, ongoing since 2017, has damaged more than 360 million cubic meters of standing timber across Germany, Austria, and the Czech Republic—an amount exceeding two years’ worth of harvest. This has resulted in a temporary influx of beetle-damaged timber, but has not alleviated high prices or supply shortages for healthy sawlogs. Sawmills in Germany are currently applying a €20 per cubic meter discount to beetle-damaged wood compared to standard BC grade prices, reflecting the lower quality and market preference for undamaged timber. Despite salvage operations, the overall supply of sustainable, high-quality logs remains insufficient to meet sawmill demand in early 2026.

## The Bark Beetle Crisis and Supply Distortion

One cannot consider these soaring prices without addressing the catastrophic spruce bark beetle outbreak that has ravaged Central European forests since 2017. This outbreak, compounded by climate change-induced stress on trees, has damaged over 360 million cubic meters of standing timber — equivalent to more than two years’ worth of harvest. Germany, Austria, and the Czech Republic have borne the brunt of this ecological crisis. [...] What at first may seem like a temporary flood of beetle-damaged timber onto the market has not eased prices, as one might expect under classical supply-demand theory. Instead, demand for healthy, untouched sawlogs remains insatiable, and the decline in the quality and quantity of sustainable timber is intensifying competition for the best logs.

The bark beetle crisis has not only triggered immediate supply shocks but also cast long shadows over the future of European forestry. While salvage operations temporarily boosted the availability of sawlogs, the ongoing decline in forest health and delays in natural regeneration threaten a consistent timber supply over the long term. Query: How has the German new housing construction sector performed in early 2026?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — German new housing construction is set to fall below 200,000 completed units in 2026, marking a severe contraction despite a modest uptick in building permits at the start of the year.

According to reporting from the Wohnungsbau-Tag 2026 industry summit, experts expect the number of newly built homes in Germany to drop below 200,000 for the year—less than half the volume considered necessary to meet demand. The event summary describes the housing market as "frozen," with high pressure and a lack of available units for new households, as highlighted by Arnt von Bodelschwingh of RegioKontext. The sector is described as being in a state of crisis, with the shortage of new construction directly impacting both mobility and economic strength.

Official data from the Statistisches Bundesamt (Destatis) confirms a slight improvement in early 2026 building activity: in January, 19,500 housing units were approved nationwide, an increase of 8.4% (or 1,500 units) compared to January 2025. Of these, 16,400 were new builds (+7.4% or +1,100 units year-on-year), and 3,100 resulted from conversions of existing buildings (+13.5% or +400 units). Within new construction, permits for single-family homes rose by 12.6% to 3,800 units, two-family homes by 26.1% to 1,200 units, and multi-family homes by 7.1%.

Industry commentary from the GdW housing association acknowledges this modest permit growth as a "slight revival" at the start of 2026, but notes that the increase in multi-family home permits—crucial for housing supply—remains subdued at 7.1%. The association calls for improved policy conditions to address the ongoing supply crisis.

The drop to under 200,000 new completions in 2026, despite January's 8.4% year-on-year permit increase, signals that the sector remains in deep contraction and that the recent uptick in permits has not yet translated into a reversal of the broader downward trend.

Query: What are the main factors driving construction cost increases in Germany?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — Construction costs in Germany remain elevated in early 2026, driven by energy price shocks from the Iran war, persistent inflation in building materials and finishing work, and ongoing regulatory and policy uncertainty.

According to the Statistische Bundesamt, prices for new conventionally built residential buildings in Germany rose by 3.2% year-on-year as of November 2025. The cost pressure is particularly acute in finishing work, with prices for these services up 3.8% over the same period. Heating and central water heating systems, such as heat pumps, saw the sharpest increase, rising by 4.4% year-on-year in November 2025.

Multiple sources confirm that the Iran war has introduced significant new uncertainty into the construction sector, primarily through its impact on energy prices and the risk of further interest rate hikes. Handelsblatt and ZDF report that rising energy costs and the potential for higher financing costs are major contributors to the current cost environment, with the geopolitical situation cited as a key risk factor for both material prices and project financing.

Regulatory and policy factors also play a role. While some local governments, such as Dresden's city council, have moved to simplify permitting processes in an effort to stimulate construction, experts cited in Handelsblatt warn that without broader reforms and a reduction in costly building requirements, affordable housing will remain scarce and construction costs will stay high.

Statistical data and industry reporting confirm that the combination of energy price shocks from the Iran war, inflation in key building components, and regulatory hurdles are the primary drivers of construction cost increases in Germany as of early 2026.

Query: What challenges are German sawmills facing in 2026?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — German sawmills in early 2026 are facing an acute raw material shortage, with timber prices at record highs and warehouses running empty, threatening the sector with an existential crisis.

Sources confirm that German timber prices for spruce and pine have reached unprecedented levels in 2026, with the timber industry raising prices significantly at the start of the year. Forest farmers are receiving higher revenues for coniferous roundwood than ever before, but sawmills are struggling with empty warehouses and skyrocketing procurement costs. The German Federal Association of the Wood Industry describes the current situation as a “raw material shortage,” warning that if more wood does not enter the market soon, the crisis could escalate into an “existential crisis for the sawmill and timber industry.”

The shortage is not limited to high-quality logs; lower-quality wood, industrial wood, and energy wood are also experiencing price surges due to the scarcity. Industrial wood, in particular, has seen the largest percentage price increases, as many sawmills now use their own sawmill residues for pellet production and must purchase additional industrial wood on the open market, further driving up prices.

Sawmills are also contending with weak demand from the construction sector, which remains the primary sales market for wood products. Although building permits have risen slightly, the overall level is still well below previous years, and no real trend reversal is evident. The combination of high raw material costs and subdued demand is placing additional strain on sawmill operations.

Empty warehouses are widespread, with reports from February 2026 indicating that many sawmills have “gähnend leer” (completely empty) inventories due to continued low deliveries of fresh logs. The Bundesverband der Holzindustrie (Federal Association of the Wood Industry) has labeled the situation as “Rohstoffunterversorgung” (raw material under-supply), reiterating the risk of an existential crisis if supply does not improve imminently.

Timber prices at record highs and acute raw material shortages make securing new log supply contracts the immediate operational priority for German sawmills.

## France and Scandinavia Also Feel the Squeeze

The French softwood sawmilling industry is in a similar situation to Germany and Austria, although the construction industry has not been hit as hard as in Germany. The French construction industry is not expecting a recovery before the 2027 presidential elections. French log prices are also increasing, albeit with a delay compared to Germany and Austria.

Traditionally more stable, Scandinavia has not escaped this squeeze, with log prices similarly elevated, while in Sweden sawlog prices have reached unsustainable levels of up to 1,800 SEK, pushing the industry deep into losses. The pulpwood segment, vital to the paper and fiber sector, has experienced comparable escalation, with spruce pulpwood deliveries surpassing €54 per cubic meter. [...] At the heart of this price surge lies a stark reality: timber supply is under acute pressure. Across much of Europe — from southern Germany and Austria to the Scandinavian countries of Sweden and Finland — spruce and pine log prices have climbed to heights not seen since the 2007–2008 boom. By mid-2025, spruce logs in southern Germany and Austria were trading between €110 and €117 per cubic meter, with northern Germany achieving even higher levels of around €115 to €120.

## France and Scandinavia Also Feel the Squeeze [...] Industry forecasts indicate that damaged wood volumes will decline by 10–20% annually through 2025, nudging harvest volumes back toward average levels yet maintaining high price pressures. This tension between supply and demand illustrates a fundamental dilemma: how to balance economic imperatives with ecological realities.

## Margin Compression and Industry Consolidation

Once viewed as natural beneficiaries of elevated raw material prices in periods of steady demand, the sawmilling industry now faces the paradox of margin compression. The cost of raw logs has increased more rapidly than the prices mills can secure for finished timber products, eroding profitability, particularly among small and medium-sized operators.

Query: What is the current state of the German real estate market and how does it affect timber demand?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — German residential building permits rose 10.8% to 238,500 units in 2025, signaling a construction sector rebound that is directly increasing timber demand and contributing to record-high wood prices in early 2026.

According to preliminary data from Statistisches Bundesamt (Destatis), Germany approved 238,500 homes in 2025, a 10.8% increase — or 23,200 more units — compared to 2024, which had marked the lowest permit level since 2010. Within new residential buildings, 194,200 units were approved, up 13.2% year-on-year, with single-family home permits rising sharply by 17.2% to 44,500 units. This marks the first annual increase in residential building permits since 2021, and industry sources such as the German Macroeconomic Policy Institute (IMK) describe this as a strong signal of sector recovery. The rise in permits is attributed to new legislation passed in October 2025 that expedited planning and approval processes, allowing municipalities to approve projects deviating from the Building Code if they serve new residential construction.

Despite this rebound in permits, the real estate association GdW expects completions to drop to just 200,000 apartments in 2026, well below the estimated annual need of 300,000 units. Cost pressures remain acute, with the average price per square meter of new living space in major cities reaching €4,630, or €5,400 including land costs, according to Dietmar Walberg of the Arge institute. Nevertheless, improving affordability and mortgage availability, alongside government subsidy programs and efforts to reduce bureaucracy, are expected to sustain positive momentum in the housing market through 2026.

This recovery in construction activity is already impacting timber demand. The residential construction sector is the largest end-use segment for treated construction timber in Germany, and sources confirm that demand for timber is closely linked to the volume and type of construction activity. As the housing market stabilizes and previously deferred demand re-enters the market in 2026, timber demand is experiencing steady, demand-led growth. Industry forecasts project an 8.3% compound annual growth rate for the German softwood timber market from 2026 to 2033, driven by rising construction, green building initiatives, and export opportunities.

On the supply side, the surge in demand has contributed to an acute raw material shortage and record-high wood prices in early 2026. Prices for spruce and pine are higher than ever before, with sawmills facing empty warehouses and significantly increased costs. Forest owners are receiving unprecedented revenues for coniferous roundwood, and even lower-quality wood grades are benefiting from the shortage. The tight market supply and rising prices are directly linked to the rebound in construction activity and the resulting increase in timber demand.

Spruce and pine prices reached all-time highs in Q1 2026 as sawmills faced acute raw material shortages, a direct result of the sharp rebound in residential building permits and construction activity.

Query: What are the latest developments in German housing construction approvals in early 2026?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — German housing construction permits rose 8.4% year-on-year in January 2026, reaching 19,500 units, marking the first significant increase after several years of decline.

According to the Federal Statistical Office (Destatis), 19,500 housing units were approved in January 2026, an increase of 8.4% or 1,500 permits compared to January 2025. Of these, 16,400 units were new builds, up 7.4% or 1,100 units year-on-year, while conversions and extensions in existing buildings accounted for 3,100 units, a 13.5% or 400-unit rise.

The breakdown shows that permits for new single-family homes climbed 12.6% to 3,800 units, and two-family homes saw a 26.1% increase to 1,200 units. Multifamily dwellings, which represent the largest segment, also contributed to the overall growth, supporting a continued upward trend in both single- and multifamily housing approvals.

Sources note that this improvement follows a prolonged downturn in the German residential construction sector. However, several reports highlight emerging risks: the ongoing Iran conflict has led to rising energy prices and the potential for higher interest rates, both of which could dampen the positive momentum seen at the start of 2026.

Housing permits in Germany increased by 8.4% year-on-year to 19,500 units in January 2026 — the first clear sign of recovery in residential construction, but ongoing geopolitical and economic uncertainties warrant close monitoring of subsequent monthly data.

Query: What is the most recent news about US lumber tariffs and their impact on European timber markets?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — In February 2026, the United States introduced a new import surcharge and continued restrictions on low-value shipments, creating a more complex and costly environment for European timber exporters.

According to a February 2026 report by WTS Global, the U.S. implemented a series of significant trade measures affecting all exporters to the country, including European timber businesses. These changes are distinct from previous tariff escalations, as they combine a major Supreme Court ruling with a new Section 122 import surcharge and the ongoing application of restrictions on low-value shipments. The policy environment is described as more unpredictable and expensive for European exporters, with the Section 122 surcharge potentially subject to amendment, extension, or replacement before July 2026.

No source provides a specific figure for the new surcharge or quantifies its direct impact on timber prices, but the WTS Global analysis highlights the need for European businesses to consider supply chain adjustments, such as U.S. or near-shore finishing and alternative logistics models, to mitigate increased costs and disruptions. The report also notes that the policy landscape remains fluid, with further changes possible in the coming months.

Available reporting does not directly link these U.S. tariff changes to immediate price movements in European timber markets, nor does it provide evidence of a direct causal impact on European timber prices as of March 2026. However, the increased complexity and cost for exporters are expected to influence supply chain strategies and could affect market dynamics if the surcharges remain or intensify.

WTS Global confirms the Section 122 surcharge introduction in February 2026, and the potential for further policy changes before July 2026 is cited as a key factor shaping European timber exporters' strategies.

Query: How do rising European Central Bank interest rates affect German housing construction demand and in turn timber consumption?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — Rising European Central Bank interest rates have pushed German mortgage rates above 4% in early 2026, dampening housing construction demand and leading to muted timber consumption.

According to ING Think, German mortgage rates climbed to their highest levels since mid-2024 in January 2026, exceeding 4% after a sharp sell-off in government bond markets triggered by Middle East tensions and rising energy prices. This increase in financing costs has directly dampened demand for new housing, with sources noting that the brief relief in February from lower capital market rates was quickly reversed. The ING analysis confirms that after a solid rebound in 2025, momentum in the German housing market softened again in early 2026 due to these interest rate pressures.

Reporting from Handelsblatt on March 18, 2026, further highlights that while there has been a recent uptick in building permits, the prospect of further ECB rate hikes—especially in the context of geopolitical uncertainty—remains a significant risk to sustained growth in German residential construction. The article explicitly links the threat of rising interest rates to a potential slowdown in housing demand, despite some positive signals from permit data.

On the timber consumption side, Fastmarkets' January 2026 report notes that German market activity for softwood timber remained muted, with limited demand from domestic construction. While spruce sawfalling 44-50×150 mm edged up by 3.17% compared to December’s midpoint, other grades held flat, and Nordic suppliers reported limited exposure to the German market. This subdued timber demand is consistent with the dampened housing construction activity attributed to higher borrowing costs.

German mortgage rates exceeding 4% in early 2026—driven by ECB policy and bond market reactions—have directly constrained housing construction demand, which in turn has resulted in muted timber consumption as confirmed by both construction and timber market sources.

Query: How does bark beetle damage in German forests affect sawmill production and ultimately timber prices?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — Bark beetle damage has caused severe timber shortages in Germany, forcing sawmills to pay record prices for quality logs and accept lower-grade wood, directly driving up timber prices in early 2026.

The catastrophic spruce bark beetle outbreak, ongoing since 2017, has damaged over 360 million cubic meters of standing timber across Central Europe, including Germany. This volume is equivalent to more than two years’ worth of harvest, according to industry reporting. While salvage operations have temporarily increased the supply of beetle-damaged wood, the overall quality and quantity of sustainable, healthy sawlogs have declined, intensifying competition among sawmills for the best logs.

Sawmills in Germany are currently paying between €130 and €135 per cubic meter for fixed-length B/C grade and B grade long spruce timber in the 2b+ thickness class, with some regional prices for Fichte (BC 2b+) reaching over €145/Fm, and in Nordrhein-Westfalen approaching €150/Fm. For 2b+ in B/C grades, prices are around €96–100 per cubic meter. Beetle-damaged wood is discounted by €20 per cubic meter off the BC price, but even lower-quality logs are being accepted by sawmills due to acute shortages.

Despite weak construction demand and stagnation in key export markets like North America and China, timber prices in Germany have continued to rise steeply in Q1 2026. Sawmills are concerned about insufficient inventories after the harvesting season and are using high prices to incentivize forest owners to supply more wood. The scarcity of healthy timber, driven by bark beetle damage, has led to sawmills facing sharply rising input costs and has forced them to accept poorer quality logs than usual.

Spruce B 2b+ at €145/Fm in March 2026 — up €5–10/Fm in Q1 — is directly linked by sources to bark beetle-driven shortages reducing sawmill production options and inflating timber prices.
context_1:
## The Bark Beetle Crisis and Supply Distortion

One cannot consider these soaring prices without addressing the catastrophic spruce bark beetle outbreak that has ravaged Central European forests since 2017. This outbreak, compounded by climate change-induced stress on trees, has damaged over 360 million cubic meters of standing timber — equivalent to more than two years’ worth of harvest. Germany, Austria, and the Czech Republic have borne the brunt of this ecological crisis. [...] What at first may seem like a temporary flood of beetle-damaged timber onto the market has not eased prices, as one might expect under classical supply-demand theory. Instead, demand for healthy, untouched sawlogs remains insatiable, and the decline in the quality and quantity of sustainable timber is intensifying competition for the best logs.

The bark beetle crisis has not only triggered immediate supply shocks but also cast long shadows over the future of European forestry. While salvage operations temporarily boosted the availability of sawlogs, the ongoing decline in forest health and delays in natural regeneration threaten a consistent timber supply over the long term. [...] ## Climate Change and the Future of Forest Management

Moreover, this crisis invites broader reflection on climate change's tangible impacts on ecosystems that industries rely upon. The bark beetle epidemic exemplifies a feedback loop where climate stress exacerbates pest outbreaks, which in turn degrade forest health and reduce resource availability.

It underscores the urgency for adaptive forest management practices that can mitigate such risks while supporting the industry's longevity.

## Conclusion

In sum, Europe’s sawmilling sector is at a crossroads shaped by record-high log prices, ecological upheavals, and shifting industry dynamics. These factors compel a reevaluation of how raw material markets operate within an ecological context and what production level Europe can uphold.

## Sägewerke unter Druck: droht Produktionsstopp?

Die Folgen treffen die Industrie direkt. Sägewerke klagen bundesweit über Rundholzmangel. Viele Betriebe nehmen verstärkt Kiefer ins Sortiment auf, um fehlende Fichte zu ersetzen. Branchenvertreter warnen bereits vor Abstellungen (Produktionsstopps) in den Sägewerken, sollte die Versorgungslage angespannt bleiben. [...] Besonders drastisch ist die Lage beim Nadelindustrieholz, das die größten prozentualen Preissteigerungen verzeichnet. Da viele Sägewerke ihr Sägerestholz mittlerweile selbst für die eigene Pelletproduktion nutzen, müssen sie Industrieholz am Markt zukaufen, was die Preise treibt.

## Holzmarkt: Bau bleibt Schlüssel – kaum Impulse aus dem Ausland

Für die Holzbranche bleibt der Bau der wichtigste Absatzmarkt. Zwar steigen die Baugenehmigungen zuletzt leicht, doch das Niveau bleibt deutlich unter früheren Jahren. Query: How do German building permit numbers in early 2026 compare to the same period in 2025?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — German building permits in January 2026 rose by 8.4% compared to January 2025, reaching 19,500 units.

According to Holzkurier and Finanznachrichten, Germany approved 19,500 new housing units in January 2026, representing an 8.4% increase over the same month in 2025. This marks the first significant year-on-year rise in building permits after several years of declines. The increase was driven primarily by new construction, with permits for new-build housing up 7.4% to 16,400 units and conversions of existing buildings up 13.5% to 3,100 units.

Segmented data shows that single-family house permits rose by 12.6%, two-family houses by 26.1%, and multi-family buildings—the largest segment—by 7.1% year-on-year. The upward trend in permits is confirmed across multiple sources, including Fordaq and BRG Building Solutions, which also note that 2025 saw a 10.8% rise in total residential permits compared to 2024, indicating a broader sector turnaround.

The spread between January 2026 and January 2025 building permits is 8.4%, or 1,500 additional units, with the strongest growth in single- and two-family house segments.

Building permit figures are widely seen as an early indicator of upcoming construction activity. Meanwhile, the trade union-linked German Macroeconomic Policy Institute (IMK) described the rise in permits as a strong signal that the housing sector is beginning to recover.
ground_truth: German building permits in January 2026 totaled 19,500 units, up 8.4% or 1,500 units compared to January 2025. New build permits rose 7.4% to 16,400 units and conversions rose 13.5% to 3,100 units. Single family homes rose 12.6%, two-family homes rose 26.1%, and multi-family homes rose 7.1% in January 2026. For full year 2025, Germany approved 238,500 housing units, up 10.8% compared to 2024 when approvals had dropped to their lowest level since 2010. The January 2026 increase marks the continuation of the upward trend that began in 2025.

Query: How does the German Heating Act impact demand for wood pellets compared to heat pumps?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — Heat pump sales in Germany overtook gas boilers in 2025, but wood pellet heating remains the lowest-cost option for typical households according to 2024/2025 data.

According to a report by consultancy co2online, heat pump sales in Germany reached approximately 284,000 units in 2025, surpassing new gas boiler installations, which stood at around 230,000 units. This marks a significant shift in the German heating market, with heat pumps becoming the standard for new buildings as the country pursues its climate neutrality goal for 2045. The German Heating Act and related policy measures are driving this transition, particularly in new construction, while the modernisation of existing building stock remains slower.

Cost comparisons from the 2025 heating index show that for a 70 m² apartment in the 2024 billing year, average annual heating costs were €680 for heat pumps and €615 for wood pellets. For a typical single-family home (140 m², 20,000 kWh heat requirement), annual costs were about €1,400 for a heat pump (SPF 3.5, 25 ct/kWh tariff) and proportionally higher for fossil fuel systems. These figures indicate that wood pellets remain the lowest-cost heating option among the main alternatives, even as heat pumps gain market share.

The spread between annual heating costs for wood pellets and heat pumps in a 70 m² apartment is €65, with wood pellets being cheaper as of the 2024 billing year.

Query: What are the current softwood lumber prices in Germany
Response:
**Headline Finding** — German softwood lumber prices have reached record highs in early 2026, with spruce (Fichte) B 2b+ trading between €125 and over €145/Fm depending on region, and pine (Kiefer) exceeding €100/Fm in many areas.

According to reporting from March 2026, buyers are paying between €125 and over €145/Fm for spruce (Fichte BC 2b+), with a pronounced north-south price gradient: in North Rhine-Westphalia, prices are nearing €150/Fm, while in northern Germany, they generally remain below €130/Fm. Pine (Kiefer) prices have also risen sharply, now surpassing the €100/Fm threshold in many regions.

Sources confirm that these price increases are occurring despite weak construction activity and stagnant export markets in North America and China. The main driver is a severe shortage of available timber, resulting in sawmills facing empty inventories and escalating procurement costs. This acute supply shortage has led to record-high prices for both spruce and pine, benefiting even lower-quality assortments and industrial wood.

Spruce B 2b+ at €125–145/Fm and pine above €100/Fm as of March 2026 — up sharply year-on-year — makes securing supply contracts at current levels the immediate operational priority.
context_1:
Für Fichte (BC 2b+) zahlten Käufer je nach Region zwischen 125 und über 145 €/Fm. Es zeigt sich ein deutliches Nord-Süd-Gefälle: Während in Nordrhein-Westfalen die Marke von 150 €/Fm kurz vor dem Reißen steht, erreicht das Preisniveau im Norden Deutschlands die 130 €/Fm in der Regel nicht. Auch die Kiefer hat deutlich zugelegt und überschreitet vielerorts die 100-€/Fm-Schwelle.

Diese Preisentwicklung überrascht. Denn sowohl die deutsche Wirtschaft als auch die internationalen Märkte liefern kaum Impulse. Die Baukonjunktur bleibt schwach, wichtige Absatzmärkte wie Nordamerika und China stagnieren. Eigentlich müssten die Preise unter diesen Bedingungen sinken. Doch das Gegenteil passiert – aus einem einfachen Grund: Es fehlt Holz.

Query: What is the latest update on EU Deforestation Regulation implementation deadlines for German timber companies?
Response:
**Headline Finding** — The practical application deadline for the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) has been postponed by one year to December 30, 2026, for German timber companies.

According to Holz-Zentralblatt (published January 9, 2026), all companies now have until December 30, 2026, to implement the EUDR requirements, following a one-year postponement. This extension is intended to give the EU Commission additional time to improve the IT system supporting EUDR implementation. The changes also introduce simplifications for small private forest owners in the EU, who will now only need to submit a one-time simplified declaration. Furthermore, only companies placing relevant products on the EU market for the first time—such as EU forest owners and importers—are required to submit due diligence statements, while subsequent traders and marketers are exempt.

A report from PSQR (March 3, 2026) confirms that the new compliance deadlines extend into late 2026 and mid-2027, but emphasizes that operational preparations remain complex. Companies must still develop capabilities for supplier information collection, geolocation data capture, risk assessment standardization, and integration of compliance workflows. The scope of the regulation has also been narrowed, with printed products removed from coverage, focusing EUDR on core forest-risk commodities and derived goods.

CMS (January 8, 2026) notes that a six-month transition period without sanctions began in January 2026, pending final approval by the European Parliament and Council. Until the proposal is formally adopted, the original compliance start date of December 2025 technically remains in force, but the practical effect is the new December 30, 2026, deadline.

December 30, 2026, is now the operative EUDR compliance deadline for German timber companies, but ongoing legislative review and the upcoming European Commission impact report (due April 30, 2026) could further clarify or adjust requirements.

Die Änderungen zielen auf Vereinfachungen für den Kleinprivatwald in den EU-Ländern. Diese Eigentümer sollen nun nur noch eine einmalige vereinfachte Erklärung abgeben müssen. # European Commission proposes adjustments to the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR)

On October 22, 2025 the European Commission presented a proposal to modify the implementation timeline of the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR). According to this new proposal, large and medium-sized companies would need to comply as of December 2025, while micro and small enterprises would have an additional year, until December 2026.
ground_truth: The EU Deforestation Regulation application deadline was postponed by one year following a European Parliament vote of 405 to 242 on December 17, 2025, confirmed by the EU Council the next day and published in the Official Journal on December 23, 2025. Large and medium operators must comply by December 30, 2026. Small and micro enterprises have until June 30, 2027. Compliance requires transitioning from manual tracking to digital traceability infrastructure. A six-month transition period without sanctions beginning January 2026 was included in an earlier proposal. Forest owners are calling for further simplifications beyond the postponement.
